---
title: "Assignment 1"
---

Assignment 1 - Task 3: Video Summary and Questions

Summary: Industrial Engineering and Data Science

I watched the interview with Cem Vardar, who has a PhD from Arizona State and spent years working at companies like Intel and Carvana. The main thing he talks about is how Industrial Engineering (IE) and Data Science actually work together in the real world.

According to Vardar, being an Industrial Engineer is all about solving problems in "complex systems". He makes a very good point that Data Science isn't a separate career path, but rather a tool—like a hammer—that we use to fix business problems. He also mentions something called "Evolutionary Design." This basically means that instead of trying to replace a whole system at once (which usually fails), it is much better to make small, data-driven improvements one by one. Lastly, he emphasizes that even though technology changes fast, basic skills like SQL and Excel are still the most important things for an engineer to master

Questions

1\. Open-Ended Question: What is Cem Vardar's "Evolutionary Design" approach, and why does he suggest it instead of replacing an entire system?

-   **Answer:** It means improving a system step-by-step with small changes. He prefers this because big, total replacements are often too complicated and risky, while small improvements based on data are more likely to actually work in a real company.

2\. Multiple-Choice Question: Which of these tools does Cem Vardar say has been a "must-have" skill for over 20 years?

a\) Neural Networks b) Deep Fakes c) SQL (Structured Query Language) d) Chat GPT

-   **Answer: c) SQL**

AI Disclosure (Footnote)

I used Gemini 3 Flash to help me summarize the transcript of the Cem Vardar interview and to format the questions

<https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Fi8-phj1X1A>

## Data Analysis

In this section, the `polls_us_election_2016` dataset from the **dslabs** package is analyzed using R.

```{r}
library(dslabs)
library(dplyr)

# Load dataset
data("polls_us_election_2016")
data <- polls_us_election_2016

# Personal variables
my_first <- "Bora"
my_birth_year <- 2004

# Calculate k
k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
k

# Display rows from original dataset
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  head(data, k)
} else {
  tail(data, k)
}

# Total NA values
sum(is.na(data))

# NA values per column (top 8)
na_per_column <- sort(colSums(is.na(data)), decreasing = TRUE)
head(na_per_column, 8)

# Replace NA values
new_data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), my_birth_year + k, .)),
    across(where(is.character), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), paste0(my_first, "_", k), .)),
    across(where(is.factor), ~ {
      x <- as.character(.)
      x[is.na(x)] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
      x
    })
  )

# Display rows from new dataset
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  head(new_data, k)
} else {
  tail(new_data, k)
}

# Remaining NA values
sum(is.na(new_data))

# anyNA check
anyNA(new_data)
```

-   I also utilized Gemini to debug and correct errors in my R code.
